How the EG is built — navigation loop
1. Navigation tab
2. Enforcement Criteria
3. Queue tab
4. Loop complete
Step 1 of 4

You're on the Navigation tab — your starting point. Hover each lettered pin to learn what that column does.

→

After hovering over each letter to learn what the column does, click the highlighted "Criminal Activity" violation (column C) to jump to its definition, or use the Next button. Click the column D pin to see what each tier means.

A
Column A — Category: the overarching group
B
Column B — Sub-Category: the specific type
C
Column C — Violation: click a name → jumps to its definition
D
Column D — Tier: click to see what each tier means
ACategory: the overarching violation group.
BSub-Category: the specific violation type.
CViolation: click any name to jump to its full definition in Enforcement Criteria. Try the highlighted Criminal Activity.
DTier: click to see what each tier means.

You're in Enforcement Criteria — the master definitions tab. Every violation's full policy detail lives here. Hover each pin to learn the column.

→

After hovering over each number to learn what the column does, click the highlighted "Customer Message Review" link in the Applicable Queue column to head to the CMR queue. The other queue links work the same way in the real guide — we're just using CMR here in the example.

1
Title — the violation name
2
Definition — plain-language description
3
Identification Criteria — what must be true
4
Violation Examples — examples that qualify
5
Exclusion Examples — examples that do NOT qualify
6
Similar Policies — related violations
7
Tier — consistent across all queues
8
Applicable Queue(s) — click a queue name (e.g. CMR) to jump there
1–6The full policy picture: Title, Definition, Identification Criteria, Violation Examples, Exclusion Examples, and Similar Policies.
7Tier — now consistent across all queues. Always use the EG tier, not a KA.
8Applicable Queue(s) — lists every queue this violation applies to. Click the highlighted Customer Message Review link to continue to the CMR queue in this example.

You're on the CMR queue tab — everything you need to action the violation in this queue. Hover each pin to learn the column.

→

When you're done: click the highlighted "Criminal Activity" title to jump back to the violation's definition in Enforcement Criteria.

1
Title — the violation name; click it to jump back to the definition
2
User Type — pro or customer
3
Picklist — the request, TACK, ADMIN, and BLIST values
4
Required Comments — what to note
5
Tier — the violation's tier in this queue
6
Request — action on the request itself
7
Admin Action: H=Hold, G=Ghost, D=Delete, M=Manual, A=Auto
8
Blocklist — Yes/No, Manual or Automatic
9
Escalation — manager or Critical Escalations?
1Title — click the highlighted Criminal Activity title to jump back to the full definition in Enforcement Criteria.
2–4User Type, Picklist, and Required Comments tell you what to select and document.
5–9Tier, Request, Admin Action, Blocklist, and Escalation tell you what to do with the account and whether to escalate.
↩

Clicking the title took you back to Enforcement Criteria — the loop is complete. Hold on a moment...

✓

That's the loop

Start at Navigation or Enforcement Criteria, click through to your queue tab for the exact action, and click the violation title to return to the definition any time you need it.

Navigation tab
→
Enforcement Criteria
→
Queue tab
→

Violation Base Tier

1–2

Content removal and/or minor account action

3

Account deletion or ghosting

4–5

Manager escalations

6

Critical Escalation
